extra.reasoning: The property G(opted_out implies not sent) holds on all reachable executions. Analysis of how each flag changes: sent can only be set true by t2 (send_attempt if not opted_out); opted_out can only be set true by t1 (optout), which atomically also sets sent:=false. Key facts: (1) opted_out is monotonic — no transition ever clears it back to false. (2) Setting sent:=true (t2) requires the guard not opted_out, so it is disabled once opted_out is true. (3) The single transition that establishes opted_out:=true (t1) simultaneously sets sent:=false in the same atomic step, so no intermediate (opted_out=true, sent=true) state arises. (4) Once opted_out=true, every still-enabled transition that touches sent sets it false: t1 (optout) sets sent:=false, t15 (send_attempt if opted_out) sets sent:=false, and all stage transitions t3-t14 set sent:=false; t2 is permanently disabled. On send_attempt, t2 and t15 are mutually exclusive on the opted_out guard, so there is no race. The stage state machine (stages 0-4) never sets sent true and is irrelevant to this property. Therefore the bad state opted_out=true AND sent=true is unreachable for any event sequence (including the 7-event bound), and the invariant is maintained.
